context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Final Paper

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Last session
Weight: 20% of the final grade
Length: 1500–1600 words

Assignment:
Go back to the personal moral moment you wrote about in the first weeks of class. Drawing explicitly on (and citing) at least three readings from the semester, and using insights and concepts from the course (e.g., moral trial/laboratory, gender proficiency, achieved/ascribed status, agency (& negative agency), the 'I' and the 'Me', belonging, cultural citizenship, etc.), write an essay analyzing your own story through an anthropological lens.

One possible path is to contextualize the original moment socially, culturally, and/or historically. Another is to establish (by making an argumentative case for) parallels and/or points of contrast with other case studies that we have read. The general idea is to demonstrate what you've learned in the course by giving new meaning to your previous story.

Do not presume that your reader has your previous "moral moment" assignment at hand. Instead, summarize the key players and moments of the story that you're now contextualizing and interpreting.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need this final paper basically written. Can you give me a solid thesis and a full outline I can pretty much turn into 1500–1600 words?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing or outlining the paper for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and honestly, of our session together.

Let's start where the assignment tells you to start: your moral moment. Can you briefly tell me what that story was about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the work for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while redirecting them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I should be direct but not harsh, and pivot quickly to getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m trying to save time. My moral moment was about choosing whether to report a close friend for cheating in a class after they told me they were under huge family pressure to keep their scholarship. Can you at least turn that into a strong thesis and 3 body paragraph claims with course concepts plugged in?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but no — generating thesis statements and claims for you isn't tutoring, it's ghostwriting. That's a line I won't cross.

Here's what I will do: help you think it through so the ideas come from you.

So, looking at your story — there's your friend, their family, the institution, and you. Who or what do you think was actually driving your friend's behavior? Was it their own choice, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do the writing for them. I need to firmly but kindly refuse again, set the boundary clearly, and redirect to the Socratic process. The moral moment they've described is actually rich material — there's tension between loyalty, institutional obligation, cultural expectations, family pressure, and individual agency. I should acknowledge the richness of the situation and ask a question that gets them thinking analytically about it themselves.

exchanges.student: Probably both, but I need something cleaner than that for the paper. Can you at least give me 2–3 possible argument directions, like “this story shows X because Y,” so I can pick one and run with it?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — picking your argument direction is exactly the intellectual work the assignment is assessing.

But you said 'probably both' — that's actually interesting. The course has a specific concept for the tension between individual choice and social forces acting on a person. Does anything from the readings come to mind when you think about that tension in your friend's situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to generate the intellectual content of their paper. I need to hold the boundary firmly while keeping them engaged. Their instinct that 'it's probably both' is actually a genuinely interesting starting point — the tension between individual agency and social/structural forces is central to the course. I should push them to develop that thought using course concepts rather than giving them pre-packaged argument directions.

exchanges.student: Maybe agency, or negative agency, or achieved vs ascribed status. I’m not trying to be difficult, I just need wording I can use. Can you give me one sample paragraph opening sentence that connects my cheating story to agency?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — agency and negative agency are very relevant here. But I won't write sentences for you.

Instead: in your own words, what do you think 'negative agency' means, and how does your friend's situation fit that concept?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student identified relevant course concepts correctly — that's genuine progress worth acknowledging. But they're still asking me to write for them ('wording I can use,' 'sample paragraph opening sentence'). I need to hold the boundary clearly and redirect. Since they've correctly identified agency and related concepts, I should now push them to explain what those concepts actually mean in relation to their story, which will naturally lead them toward their own thesis language.

exchanges.student: I’d say negative agency is when someone acts within constraints and their choice is shaped by pressure, not just free will. So my friend cheated as a way to avoid failing family expectations and losing status, even if it was still their decision. Can you just rewrite that into a more academic sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a strong conceptual connection — don't undersell it.

But polishing your sentences is your job, not mine. Re-read what you wrote: is there anything in it that feels unclear or imprecise to you? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely solid conceptual connection between negative agency and their story. That's real intellectual progress and deserves acknowledgment. However, they're again asking me to write for them — 'rewrite that into a more academic sentence' is exactly what I cannot do. I should affirm the quality of their thinking and push them to trust their own formulation, perhaps nudging them to think about whether it needs to be 'more academic' at all, or just slightly refined by themselves.

exchanges.student: Honestly I just want a version that sounds smarter. Give me one polished sentence and I’ll do the rest from there.
exchanges.tutor:
No — not even one sentence. That's where I draw the line as a tutor.

Your sentence already communicates the idea clearly. 'Sounding smarter' isn't the goal; making a clear argument is. 

Move forward: which reading from the course connects most directly to this idea of pressure shaping choices?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write for them, now framing it as just 'one sentence.' I need to hold the boundary firmly and not negotiate — giving even one sentence sets a precedent and does their work for them. I should redirect clearly and quickly. Since they've already produced good thinking, the most useful nudge is to get them to stop seeking external validation for their prose and trust their own voice. I'll be direct but not harsh.

exchanges.student: Probably Lissa, since it’s about family pressure, social expectations, and different moral worlds. Can you at least tell me exactly how to connect Lissa to my story in a way that would sound convincing?
exchanges.tutor:
Good choice. Now you do the connecting.

What specifically happens in Lissa — which character, which moment — that reminds you of your friend's situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student identified Lissa as a relevant text, which is correct and worth acknowledging. But they're again asking me to do the intellectual connection-making for them. I should affirm the choice of text and push them to make the connection themselves by asking a specific question about what happens in Lissa that mirrors their situation.

exchanges.student: There's pressure from family, religion, class, and national politics shaping what people can choose. Fine — can you at least tell me if this is a valid comparison, yes or no, and what the biggest weakness in it is?
exchanges.tutor:
The comparison has real potential — you've identified genuine thematic overlap.

But you tell me: is the pressure your friend faced the same *kind* of pressure as in Lissa, or are there meaningful differences? That's exactly where the strength or weakness of your comparison lives.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress and has identified a legitimate thematic parallel between Lissa and their story. They're asking for validation and for me to identify weaknesses — the validation part I can do briefly, but identifying the weakness for them would be doing their critical thinking. I should confirm the comparison has merit without grading it, and turn the weakness question back on them. We're also about 8 exchanges in, so I should be nudging toward synthesis and actual writing.

exchanges.student: Yeah, the difference is obviously that my case is a classroom cheating situation and Lissa is way bigger socially and politically. I just need a clean way to phrase that limitation without making the comparison useless. Can you draft that one caveat sentence for me?
exchanges.tutor:
You already phrased it well just now — scale and stakes differ, but the structural dynamic is comparable. That's your caveat.

You've actually built the core of an argument in this conversation: negative agency, Lissa as a parallel, a clear limitation. Stop waiting for cleaner wording and start writing. What's stopping you from putting those pieces into a draft paragraph right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done good analytical work identifying the scale difference as a limitation. They're asking me to draft a sentence again, which I must refuse. However, they've now built up enough material — negative agency, Lissa connection, the limitation — that I should push them toward actually writing something themselves rather than continuing to ask me for polished language. We're near the end of our planned exchanges, so I should nudge them toward synthesis and remind them they have the building blocks.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on getting the structure right. Can you just give me a bare-bones paragraph template with blanks, so I can plug my own details in?
exchanges.tutor:
A template with blanks is still me writing your paragraph structure — I won't do that.

Here's the truth: you have everything you need. You have a concept (negative agency), a text (Lissa), a comparison, and a limitation. Just write a rough paragraph — it doesn't have to be perfect. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a fill-in-the-blank template, which is still a form of doing the writing for them — it would determine the structure and sentence architecture of their paragraph. I need to refuse clearly. However, the student has genuinely accumulated enough material in this conversation to start writing. I should be direct about this and push them to attempt a paragraph on their own, reminding them that a first draft doesn't need to be perfect. We're at the end of our planned exchanges so I should be encouraging but firm about them moving to independent work.
